skill.name: azure-upgrade
skill.description: Assess Azure workloads before plan, tier, or SKU upgrades, then guide safe Azure-native upgrade steps with validation and rollback planning.
skill.summary: Assess and guide Azure Functions plan upgrades with reports, automation steps, validation, and rollback guidance.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, and credential-adjacent patterns. Manual review confirms the commands are expected Azure upgrade automation, not malicious behavior, but the skill can create, modify, deploy to, and delete Azure resources and can expose app settings or storage connection strings during migration.

medium_findings.title: Azure Resource Modification Commands
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill provides Azure CLI workflows that create Flex Consumption apps, update authentication and scale settings, deploy code, and delete function apps. This is legitimate for an Azure upgrade skill, but it can change production cloud resources if used without careful confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and destructive-operation guardrails are explicit in the referenced files. Confidence is high because the behavior is real, though it is aligned with the stated upgrade purpose.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive App Settings and Storage Connection String Handling
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The automation collects and prints app settings, then reads AzureWebJobsStorage into a shell variable and uses it to download deployment packages. This may expose secrets in terminal output, shell history, logs, or copied assessment artifacts if not redacted.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly shows app settings being echoed and a storage connection string being captured. No exfiltration endpoint was found, so the risk is exposure during normal operation rather than confirmed theft.

medium_findings.title: Shell Automation Uses Unquoted Variables
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: Several bash examples pass variables such as appName, rgName, systemUserId, storageConnection, and packageName without quotes. This can cause word splitting, glob expansion, or incorrect command arguments when names or blob paths contain special characters.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell snippets visibly expand variables without quotes in Azure CLI commands. This is a realistic hardening issue, although Azure naming constraints reduce the chance of full command injection.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Dismissed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak-cryptography alerts appear to match ordinary words and Azure guidance text, such as destructive-action policy, managed identity, and upgrade descriptions. No MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or custom cryptographic implementation was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain policy and descriptive text rather than cryptographic primitives. Confidence is very high because the files are documentation and no crypto API usage appears there.

low_findings.title: PowerShell Invocation Matches Dismissed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The PowerShell alerts are references to Azure Cloud Shell compatibility and the PowerShell language stack for Azure Functions, not commands that invoke PowerShell on the host.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text lists supported runtimes and says scripts are bash. There is no pwsh or powershell command execution instruction in those locations.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs Are Benign
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Hardcoded URLs point to Microsoft Learn and Azure sample documentation. They support user research and do not receive secrets or application data from the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are clearly documentation links. No evidence was found that sensitive data is posted to these URLs.

low_findings.title: Standard Device File Access Is Benign
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The /dev/null usage appears only in curl smoke-test commands that discard response bodies while collecting HTTP status codes. This is normal shell behavior and not suspicious filesystem access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is HTTP status checking with curl output redirection. There is no read or write of user files or privileged system files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive Azure Delete Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide includes commands that delete the original or new function app. The surrounding text requires ask_user confirmation, but marketplace users should understand that these commands can remove cloud resources.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The delete commands and confirmation policy are explicit. The risk is clear even though the workflow contains mitigation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential-Adjacent App Settings Collection
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide collects all app settings and reads the AzureWebJobsStorage connection string for package download. These values often contain secrets and should be redacted from reports and logs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact setting name and shell variable are visible. The risk is sensitive data exposure, not confirmed malicious exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Network and Cloud Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow uses Azure CLI, Azure MCP tools, function app smoke tests, and documentation lookups. These operations contact Azure, Microsoft documentation, and user-owned app endpoints.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network and cloud operations are documented directly. They are legitimate for this skill but require user awareness and correct tenant selection.

content.value_statement: Azure Functions plan upgrades can affect production settings, triggers, identity, and deployment paths. This skill guides assessment, migration, validation, and rollback steps for Azure-native upgrades.
actual_capabilities: Guides Azure Functions Consumption to Flex Consumption upgrade planning.
Generates a structured upgrade assessment report before changes are made.
Provides Azure CLI steps for eligibility checks, app creation, deployment, validation, and cleanup.
Uses Azure MCP documentation and best-practice tools for current guidance.
Tracks upgrade progress in upgrade-status.md in the workspace root.
Requires user confirmation before target selection and destructive actions.
limitations: The detailed scenario covers Linux Azure Functions Consumption to Flex Consumption.
It requires Azure CLI access, correct Azure permissions, and installed helper tools.
It does not automatically migrate application code without a chosen deployment path.
It can expose app settings if users copy outputs without redaction.

use_cases.title: Assess Flex Consumption Readiness
use_cases.description: Check region support, runtime compatibility, triggers, certificates, slots, identities, and blockers before changing an Azure Functions plan.

use_cases.title: Run a Controlled Plan Upgrade
use_cases.description: Follow ordered Azure CLI steps to create the new app, migrate configuration, deploy code, and validate service health.

use_cases.title: Prepare Rollback and Cleanup
use_cases.description: Keep the original app available, document rollback actions, and only clean up old resources after validation and approval.

prompt_templates.title: Check Upgrade Fit
prompt_templates.prompt: Assess whether my Azure Function App can move from Consumption to Flex Consumption. Ask me for the app name, resource group, region, and subscription before running checks.

prompt_templates.title: Create Assessment Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an upgrade assessment report for my Function App. Include compatibility checks, app settings inventory, managed identities, trigger risks, blockers, recommendations, and next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Migration Steps
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a step-by-step migration plan from Linux Consumption to Flex Consumption. Include Azure CLI commands, confirmation points, validation checks, and rollback actions.

prompt_templates.title: Execute With Guardrails
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through the automated Flex Consumption migration. Confirm each subscription, region, resource creation, deployment method, DNS change, and cleanup action before execution.

output_examples.input: Is my production Function App ready for Flex Consumption?
output: A readiness summary with current plan, target plan, region, runtime, and upgrade status.
A compatibility table covering slots, certificates, blob triggers, .NET mode, identities, and dependencies.
A blocker and warning list with clear remediation steps before migration.

output_examples.input: Help me migrate after the assessment passes.
output: A phased migration checklist covering pre-migration capture, new app creation, deployment, smoke tests, monitoring, and rollback.
Confirmation prompts before resource creation, deployment choices, DNS changes, and cleanup.
Validation guidance for HTTP endpoints, App Insights, scale settings, and trigger behavior.

output_examples.input: What should I do after the new app is live?
output: A post-upgrade action list for client routing, monitoring, performance review, IaC updates, and old app retention.
Cleanup guidance that keeps the original app until the owner approves deletion.

best_practices: Run the assessment and resolve blockers before creating or modifying Azure resources.
Redact app settings, connection strings, principal IDs, and hostnames before sharing reports.
Keep the original app running until the new app is validated and rollback is documented.
anti_patterns: Do not skip user confirmation for subscription, region, resource creation, DNS, or cleanup actions.
Do not paste unredacted app settings or storage connection strings into tickets or chat logs.
Do not delete the original app immediately after deployment without an agreed rollback window.

faq.question: Does this skill support cross-cloud migration?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on Azure-to-Azure plan, tier, SKU, and service upgrade workflows.

faq.question: What upgrade path is documented in detail?
faq.answer: The detailed reference path is Linux Azure Functions Consumption to Azure Functions Flex Consumption.

faq.question: Will the skill delete my original app?
faq.answer: It should not delete or stop the original app without explicit user confirmation.

faq.question: Does code move automatically to the new app?
faq.answer: No. The workflow helps choose a deployment method, such as CI/CD, local publish, or an existing package.

faq.question: What permissions are required?
faq.answer: You need Azure CLI access and sufficient permissions to inspect, create, configure, deploy, and assign identities.

faq.question: How should secrets be handled?
faq.answer: Redact app settings and connection strings from reports, logs, screenshots, and shared command output.
